title: The problem lists solve
instruction:
Imagine you want to store five student names. Without lists, you would do this:

```python
student1 = "Alice"
student2 = "Bob"
student3 = "Carol"
student4 = "David"
student5 = "Eve"
```

That is painful. And what if you have 1000 students?

A list holds all of them in one variable:

`students = ["Alice", "Bob", "Carol", "David", "Eve"]`

Run this.
successMessage: One variable, five values. len() gives you the count. This is why lists exist — one container for many related values.

title: Accessing items by index
instruction:
Each item in a list has a position called an **index**, starting at `0`:

```
["Alice", "Bob", "Carol"]
   [0]     [1]    [2]
```

`students[0]` → `"Alice"` (first item)
`students[-1]` → last item (Python counts from the back with negatives)

Print the first, third, and last student.
starter:
students = ["Alice", "Bob", "Carol", "David", "Eve"]
# TODO: print first, third, and last student

codeChecks.message: Access first item: students[0]
Access third item: students[2]
Access last item: students[-1]
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Index 0 is the first item. Index -1 is always the last, regardless of list size. This is one of Python's most convenient features.

title: Modifying items
instruction:
Lists are **mutable** — you can change items after creation.

`students[1] = "Robert"` — replaces item at index 1.

Change `"Bob"` to `"Robert"` and `"Eve"` to `"Emma"`.
starter:
students = ["Alice", "Bob", "Carol", "David", "Eve"]
print("Before:", students)
# TODO: change Bob to Robert and Eve to Emma
print("After:", students)

codeChecks.message: Change students[1] to "Robert"
Change the last item to "Emma"
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Bob is now Robert, Eve is now Emma. Lists remember your changes. Unlike strings and tuples, lists are fully editable after creation.

title: append() and insert()
instruction:
Add items to a list:

- `list.append(item)` — adds to the **end**
- `list.insert(index, item)` — adds at a **specific position**

Add `"Frank"` to the end with `append()`. Then insert `"Zara"` at position 0 (first).
starter:
students = ["Alice", "Bob", "Carol"]
print("Before:", students)
# TODO: append Frank and insert Zara at position 0
print("After:", students)

codeChecks.message: Append Frank: students.append("Frank")
Insert Zara at position 0: students.insert(0, "Zara")
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: append adds at the end, insert puts it wherever you want. append is O(1) — instant. insert is O(n) — slower on large lists.

title: remove() and pop()
instruction:
Remove items from a list:

- `list.remove(value)` — removes the **first occurrence** of a value
- `list.pop(index)` — removes by **index** and **returns** the removed item
- `list.pop()` — removes the **last** item

Remove `"Bob"` by value, then pop the last item.
starter:
students = ["Alice", "Bob", "Carol", "David", "Eve"]
print("Before:", students)
# TODO: remove Bob, then pop the last item
print("After:", students)

codeChecks.message: Remove Bob: students.remove("Bob")
Pop the last item: students.pop()
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: remove deletes by value (first match only), pop deletes by index and returns the item. Both modify the list in-place.

title: Slicing — extract a portion
instruction:
Slicing extracts a portion of a list:

`list[start:stop]` — items from start up to (not including) stop
`list[:3]` — first 3 items
`list[2:]` — from index 2 to the end
`list[::2]` — every 2nd item

Extract the first three students, the last two, and every other student.
starter:
students = ["Alice", "Bob", "Carol", "David", "Eve"]
# TODO: print first 3, last 2, and every other student

codeChecks.message: Slice first 3: students[:3]
Slice last 2: students[-2:]
Every other item: students[::2]
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Slicing is one of Python's most powerful features. It creates a new list — it does not modify the original. Use it to extract any sub-sequence.

title: Useful list methods
instruction:
Lists have many built-in methods:

- `sort()` — sorts in place
- `sorted()` — returns a new sorted list
- `reverse()` — reverses in place
- `count(x)` — counts occurrences of x
- `index(x)` — finds position of x

Run this. Then add: print how many times `3` appears, and find the index of `7`.
starter:
numbers = [5, 2, 8, 3, 1, 7, 3, 9]
print(sorted(numbers))
print(numbers)  # original unchanged
numbers.sort()
print(numbers)  # now sorted in-place
# TODO: count 3s and find index of 7

codeChecks.message: Count 3s: numbers.count(3)
Find index of 7: numbers.index(7)
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: sort() changes the original, sorted() creates a new one. count and index are for searching. All built in — no extra imports needed.

title: in — check if item exists
instruction:
`in` works on lists just like it works on strings:

`"Alice" in students` → `True` if Alice is in the list.

From the last chapter you know `not in` works too.

Check if each subject is available before enrolling.
starter:
available_subjects = ["Math", "Science", "History", "English"]
wanted = ["Math", "Art", "Science"]

# TODO: for each subject in wanted, print whether it is available
# "Math: available" or "Art: not available"

codeChecks.message: Use: if subject in available_subjects:
Loop over wanted: for subject in wanted:
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: in on a list checks all elements — O(n). For fast lookups, you would use a set. But for small lists, in is perfectly fine.

title: List comprehension with conditions
instruction:
You can add a filter condition to a list comprehension:

`[x for x in numbers if x > 0]` — only positive numbers

Create two lists using comprehension:
1. All even numbers from 1–20
2. All students with names longer than 4 characters
hint:
[x for x in range(1, 21) if x % 2 == 0]
[s for s in students if len(s) > 4]
starter:
students = ["Alice", "Bob", "Carol", "David", "Eve", "Isabella"]

# TODO: list of evens 1-20
evens = []
print(evens)

# TODO: list of students with names > 4 chars
long_names = []
print(long_names)

codeChecks.message: Use comprehension with condition: [x for x in range(1,21) if x % 2 == 0]
Filter by name length: [s for s in students if len(s) > 4]
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: Filtered comprehensions in one line. This is the idiomatic Python way to create filtered lists. Clear, fast, and readable.

title: Build a grade tracker
instruction:
No starter code.

Build a grade tracker:
1. Create a list of 5 scores (e.g. `[85, 92, 78, 96, 88]`)
2. Print the highest score using `max()`
3. Print the lowest using `min()`
4. Print the average (sum divided by count)
5. Print all scores above 85 using list comprehension
hint:
avg = sum(scores) / len(scores)
high_scores = [s for s in scores if s > 85]

codeChecks.message: Use max() for the highest score.
Use min() for the lowest score.
Use sum() for calculating the average.
Use list comprehension with a condition.

successMessage: A real grade tracker — max, min, average, filtered list. You just used Python's most important data structure in a real scenario. Chapter 8 done.
